title: The Missing Martyrs: Why There Are So Few Muslim Terrorists By Charles Kurzman. Oxford University Press. 2011. 248 pages. $24.95 (cloth)
abstract: “Our fears of terrorism are exaggerated. There just aren't many [Islamic] terrorists, thank goodness.” This statement neatly sums up the essence of The Missing Martyrs, ending in lofty recommendations such as turn down the volume on terrorism debates, accept uncertainty, and be brave. The Missing Martyrs' literate author, Charles Kurzman, has assembled a mass of data from opinion polls, terrorists' websites, and (an undisclosed number of) interviews with young Muslims, in order to explain why we have seen relatively few terrorist attacks since 9/11, even after Al-Qaeda leaders called it an individual religious duty to commit attacks: “well over 99 percent ignored the call to action.” The main cause, according to Kurzman, is the dominant view among Muslims that they should abstain from violence. Many Muslims favor democracy and embrace Western values, and consequently Islamist terrorists are a marginalized, divided, and incompetent bunch, who furthermore often act amateurishly. Some of these terrorists are not even deeply religious and have very little understanding of the Quran, in the same vein as leftist terrorists in the 1970s who started reading Marx only after they were jailed. Many Islamist terrorists are primarily anti-imperialists, but the extent to which they will carry their struggle into the world is conditioned by the question of whether they belong to the globalists or the localists. Where Islamist extremists commit their attacks, the support for Al-Qaeda and its like declines even further. And even where there is support, it is “symbolic, not strategic.” Kurzman's tongue-in-cheek conclusion: “terrorism is not a leading cause of death in the world.” True, but Kurzman overlooks the fact that a death caused by terrorists does have a broader societal impact than death caused by traffic incidents or winter storms. He certainly has a point when he says that if decreasing the number of fatalities were the norm, money spent on counterterrorism could often be spent more effectively on fighting disease. Many counterradicalization strategies have addressed the question of resilience against radical Islam among Muslims, and from Kurzman's book they can learn to what extent such resilience seems already to be present. The book's final chapters, “Uncle Sam versus Uncle Usama” and “Predicting Next Attacks,” have less to do with the author's main argument, being a not very clear cut and needlessly digressive description of the impact of U.S. foreign policy in the Islamic world and a stocktaking of both Middle East and Islamic studies in the United States and the ability of social scientists to predict.
